classification.sub_class: firming agent, processing aid, pH adjuster

natural_sources: Formed when calcium oxide reacts with water
natural_occurrence.description: While calcium hydroxide can form naturally, commercial production is by reacting quicklime (calcium oxide) with water. The calcium oxide itself comes from heating limestone.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting calcium oxide (quicklime) with water in an exothermic 'slaking' reaction. The calcium oxide is obtained by heating limestone (calcium carbonate) to high temperatures. Food-grade calcium hydroxide is purified to remove impurities.

other_uses.cosmetics: Rarely used; pH adjuster in some formulations.
other_uses.industrial: Cement and mortar, water treatment, leather tanning, soil stabilization, plaster.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Dental applications (root canal treatment), antacid formulations.

eu.conditions: Permitted as processing aid and firming agent; excess is washed away or neutralized.

usa.notes: Widely used in traditional food processing, especially nixtamalization and pickling.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (acceptable for use, mostly removed during processing)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

commonly_found_in: TORTILLAS and masa (nixtamalization of corn - essential process for traditional Mexican food)
Crispy pickles (pickling lime keeps them crunchy)
fun_facts: ESSENTIAL for making authentic tortillas - the nixtamalization process with slaked lime unlocks nutrients in corn
Nixtamalization (cooking corn in lime water) has been used for 3,000+ years in Mesoamerica
Without lime treatment, corn-based diets can cause pellagra (niacin deficiency) - lime unlocks bound niacin
Called 'cal' in Spanish - you'll find it in Mexican grocery stores for making tortillas at home
The same substance used in construction as 'hydrated lime' for mortar and plaster
Makes pickles incredibly crispy by firming the pectin in cucumber cell walls
Turns yellow corn into white hominy and gives it a distinctive slightly alkaline flavor
